one_line_takeaway: SECRET is the first black-box RAG data-extraction attack that jointly optimizes an LLM-generated jailbreak prompt and an adaptive cluster-focused retrieval trigger, breaking safety-aligned commercial RA-LLMs (34% extraction from defended Claude 3.7 Sonnet, where all prior attacks score 0%) and evading every tested defense stage.
key_finding: SECRET is the first EDEA to succeed against strongly safety-aligned commercial RA-LLMs, where all prior attacks fail (0% extraction). Under naive system-prompt defenses on HealthcareMagic-101 (Table II), SECRET extracts a 34.0% MER against Claude 3.7 Sonnet (RR 7.7%) while every baseline yields 0% MER on Claude; it also reaches 48.1% MER on Gemini 2.0 Flash, 45.7% on GPT-4o mini, and 49.4% on DeepSeek-V3. Its ER-TMQ (early efficiency) is roughly 2x prior work. Two of the paper's stated takeaways: (1) leading aligned models (Claude 3.7) inherently resist vanilla EDEAs (~100% refusal), and (2) even a simple defensive system-prompt instruction neutralizes prior attacks (~0% MER) but not SECRET. The authors evaluate four defense stages (retrieval, LLM-input, LLM-output) and find none sufficient: e.g., a similarity threshold that filters ~50% of documents (tau=0.85) still leaves 24.6% ER-TMQ; output similarity detection is evaded near-100% by inserting spaces between letters; the DataSentinel prompt-injection detector only partially detects (misses Claude/DeepSeek prompts) and has a 96.1% false-positive rate on benign prompts; and Llama-Guard-3-8B plus a perplexity detector give 0% detection of SECRET's natural-language prompts.
core_contribution: The paper makes two distinct contributions of its own (as opposed to the prior EDEAs it consolidates). (1) It proposes the first unified framework for external data extraction attacks (EDEAs) against retrieval-augmented LLMs (RA-LLMs), formally defining the attack objective (maximize the number of unique documents extracted from the RAG knowledge base within a query budget) and decomposing any attack query p-hat into three functional components: an extraction instruction (pe, directs verbatim repetition), a jailbreak operator (J, transforms pe to bypass safety alignment/defensive system prompts), and a retrieval trigger (ti, steers the retriever toward yet-unextracted documents). Prior attacks (Qi et al., Zeng et al., Cohen et al., Jiang et al.) are recast as special cases within this taxonomy (Table I). (2) Guided by the framework, it builds SECRET (Scalable and EffeCtive exteRnal data Extraction aTtack), which for the first time simultaneously strengthens all three components: an LLM-driven adaptive optimizer that discovers natural jailbreak prompts under black-box access, plus cluster-focused triggering (CFT) that alternates global exploration and local exploitation to efficiently discover new documents.
threat_model: Black-box, end-user adversary interacting with a deployed RA-LLM application. The attacker does NOT know the backend LLM f, the system prompt ps, the retrieval components (embedding model E, similarity function), or the documents in the private knowledge base D. Capabilities are limited to submitting arbitrary prompts and observing responses; the attacker cannot inject documents into D, modify ps, or alter retrieval/generation logic. Objective: extract (recover verbatim, up to a relaxed distance threshold) as many unique documents from D as possible while minimizing query cost, motivated by large-scale privacy violation or IP theft against enterprise/medical/financial RAG deployments. The authors note this is stricter than prior work that assumed partial DB knowledge (Qi et al., Huang et al.) or access to retrieval components (Cohen et al.).
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) restricted to text-only retrieval; multimodal RAG is out of scope and unexamined. (2) A gap remains between empirical ER-TMQ and the 100% theoretical optimum, i.e., non-redundant full coverage under black-box conditions is still an open search problem. Reviewer-noted: (3) 'Successful extraction' uses a permissive normalized-Levenshtein threshold (tau=0.1), so reported rates reflect near-verbatim rather than strictly identical reproduction (the authors justify this to avoid false negatives). (4) Databases are 1,000-entry subsamples, not full-scale corpora, and results center on HealthcareMagic-101 (Enron results relegated to appendix). (5) The abstract headlines '35% of the data from RAG powered by Claude 3.7 Sonnet,' but the corresponding main-table figure under naive defense is 34.0% MER (Table II); the 35% figure is not pinned to a specific table cell in the main text. (6) Some defenses (e.g., summarization preprocessing) do eliminate verbatim extraction (0% ER-TMQ) at a utility cost, so the 'no effective defense' claim is about verbatim leakage; semantic leakage remains high (sem. sim. 0.911).

datasets_used: HealthcareMagic-101 (1,000 sampled medical Q&A entries) - primary evaluation dataset
Enron Email (1,000 sampled entries) - secondary, results mostly in appendix
WikiText-103 - used as public 'noise' corpus in the data-mixing defense experiment
Wikipedia / external corpora - source of random natural-text chunks for Global Exploration triggers
awesome-chatgpt-prompts - benign prompts used to measure DataSentinel's false-positive rate
quantitative_results: Claude 3.7 Sonnet, naive defense, HealthcareMagic (Table II): SECRET RR 7.7%, ER-TMQ 18.9%, MER 34.0%; all 5 baselines 0% MER
Gemini 2.0 / GPT-4o mini / DeepSeek-V3, naive defense (Table II): SECRET MER 48.1% / 45.7% / 49.4%
No-defense setting (Table III): SECRET RR 0% on all 4 models, ER-TMQ 25.5-30.4% (~2x prior work), MER 36.5-49.1%
Ablation (Table IV, GPT-4o mini, HealthcareMagic): GE-only baseline 100% RR / 0% MER; +optimized pe* 32.5% MER; GE+LE+SS 52.2% MER; full with priority queue 28.1% ER-TMQ / 54.2% MER
Retrieval similarity-threshold defense: at tau=0.85 (~50% of relevant docs filtered) SECRET still achieves 24.6% ER-TMQ

Context preprocessing (Table VII): rephrase drops ER-TMQ 28.8%->21.6%, MER 45.5%->36.7% but semantic similarity stays 0.974; summarize gives 0% verbatim extraction, semantic similarity 0.911, utility 0.196
DataSentinel prompt-injection detector: partial detection (misses Claude/DeepSeek-targeted prompts) with 96.1% false-positive rate on benign prompts
Output-stage similarity detection (Table IX, threshold 0.6): bypassed to ~0% detection rate by inserting spaces between letters, ER-TMQ nearly unchanged
Stealth: Llama-Guard-3-8B and perplexity detector both yield 0% detection across all target models
Jailbreak-optimization overhead: <16 target queries on average for Ncand in [3,7]
Data-mixing defense (Fig 9): ER-TMQ stays 21.3-23.6% across 1:0.2 to 1:1 private:public ratios; MER falls 44.2%->33.5%
SECRET against bare LLMs without RAG: 0% extraction (confirms leakage comes from retrieved context, not pretraining memorization)
baselines_compared: Zeng et al. (Findings of ACL 2024) - simple repetition + random sampling trigger
Qi et al. (ICLR 2025, 'Follow My Instruction and Spill the Beans') - simple repetition + random sampling
Cohen et al. (arXiv 2024) - simple repetition + static role-play jailbreak template + discrete-optimization trigger
Jiang et al. (RAG-Thief, arXiv 2024) - LLM-based adaptive trigger generation
GCG Leak - the authors' own adaptation of GCG (Zou et al. 2023) to the extraction task
Additionally tested and found ineffective for EDEAs (Appendix C case study on Claude 3.7): PAP, LLM-Fuzzer, and the PLeak prompt-stealing attack
